title: Geriatric Anesthesia MCQ – Chapter 30
source: Short Textbook of Anesthesia – Ajay Yadav

questions.question: What is the most common cardiac finding in old age?
questions.explanation: Page 242: Decreased ventricular compliance leading to diastolic dysfunction is the most common finding in old age. It is also a significant determinant of morbidity.

questions.question: What cardiac function is usually preserved in old age despite other cardiovascular changes?
questions.explanation: Page 242: Systolic function usually remains preserved in old age, whereas diastolic dysfunction is the most common finding due to decreased ventricular compliance.

questions.question: What happens to blood pressure with advancing age?

options.A: Decreases due to reduced cardiac output

questions.explanation: Page 242: Blood pressure increases with age. This along with decreased heart rate, decreased adrenergic response, and decreased cardiac reserve increase the incidence of cardiac complications.

questions.question: Which respiratory change in old age leads to V/Q mismatch and decreased pO2?
questions.explanation: Page 242: Increase in closing capacity increases the V/Q mismatch decreasing the pO2. This is due to decreased elasticity leading to alveolar distension.

questions.question: Which spinal deformities can cause difficulty in ventilation in geriatric patients?
questions.explanation: Page 242: Kyphosis and scoliosis can cause difficulty in ventilation in geriatric patients.

questions.question: Why is the cough reflex decreased in old age patients?

options.A: Decreased respiratory muscle function decreases the cough reflex

questions.explanation: Page 242: Decreased respiratory muscle function decreases the cough reflex in geriatric patients, making them more prone to aspiration pneumonia.

questions.question: What is the CNS consequence of loss of neurons in old age?

options.D: Decreased brain reserve leading to dementia

questions.explanation: Page 242: Loss of neurons decreases the brain reserve leading to dementia. There also occurs increased sensitivity to anesthetic medications.

questions.question: In which age group is perioperative delirium and postoperative cognitive dysfunction (POCD) far more common?
questions.explanation: Page 242: The risk of perioperative delirium and postoperative cognitive dysfunction (POCD) is far more common in old age people due to decreased brain reserve.

questions.question: What thermoregulation risk is the geriatric population more prone to?
questions.explanation: Page 242: Geriatric population is more prone for hypothermia due to impaired thermoregulation mechanisms.

questions.question: Is age a contraindication for surgery?

options.A: Age is not a contraindication for any surgery
options.C: Yes, age > 70 is a relative contraindication

questions.explanation: Page 242: Age is not a contraindication for any surgery. Chronological age is neither a contraindication for any surgery nor a criterion to do battery of routine investigations.

questions.question: Which preoperative assessment is a MUST for all geriatric patients?
questions.explanation: Page 242: Assessment of cognitive function is must. Patients with preoperative dementia have higher incidence of postoperative delirium.

questions.question: What is the significance of preoperative dementia in geriatric patients?

options.B: Patients with preoperative dementia have higher incidence of postoperative delirium

questions.explanation: Page 242: Patients with preoperative dementia have higher incidence of postoperative delirium. Moreover, cognitive assessment can be used to compare if patient develops cognitive function in the postoperative period.

questions.question: In geriatric patients, what determines when investigations should be done preoperatively?

options.A: All patients need a complete battery of tests
options.B: Investigations should be guided by associated comorbidity

questions.explanation: Page 242: Age is not a criteria to do battery of routine investigations. Investigations should be guided by associated comorbidity.

questions.question: Why must a detailed record of medication be obtained in geriatric patients preoperatively?

options.C: Many old age people are on polypharmacy and appropriate instructions must be given

questions.explanation: Page 242: Many of the old age people are on polypharmacy. A detailed record of medication should be obtained and instructions are given accordingly.

questions.question: Which type of anesthesia is PREFERRED over general anesthesia in geriatric patients?
questions.explanation: Page 242: Regional anesthesia is preferred over general anesthesia due to increased sensitivity of brain to anesthetic agents, decreased metabolism/excretion, reduced chances of drug toxicity, decreased thromboembolism risk, fewer pulmonary complications, and less incidence of postoperative delirium.

questions.question: Which benefit of regional anesthesia is related to reduced postoperative venous thrombosis?

options.D: Central neuraxial blocks decrease the risk of thromboembolism

questions.explanation: Page 243: Central neuraxial blocks decrease the risk of thromboembolism. This is one reason regional anesthesia is preferred over general anesthesia in geriatric patients.

questions.question: Why may central neuraxial blocks be technically difficult in geriatric patients?

options.C: Due to degenerative changes in spine

questions.explanation: Page 243: Regional anesthesia, particularly central neuraxial blocks, may be technically difficult due to degenerative changes in spine.

questions.question: Why do old age patients achieve a higher level of spinal and epidural block?

options.A: Due to increased drug sensitivity only
options.B: Due to narrowing of spaces in the spine

questions.explanation: Page 243: Old age patients achieve higher level of spinal (and epidural) due to narrowing of spaces. Geriatric population is also more sensitive to the effect of local anesthetics during nerve blocks.

questions.question: By how much should benzodiazepine premedication requirement be reduced in geriatric patients?
questions.explanation: Page 243: As the requirement of benzodiazepines may be reduced by 50%, premedication with benzodiazepines should be done cautiously in geriatric patients.

questions.question: Why is the dose of ALL IV induction agents decreased in geriatric patients?

options.C: Only due to reduced cardiac output
options.D: Increased brain sensitivity, decreased volume of distribution, decreased unbound fraction (low albumin), reduced metabolism and clearance

questions.explanation: Page 243: Increase sensitivity of brain, decrease volume of distribution, decreased unbound fraction (due to decreased albumin levels) and reduced metabolism and clearance not only decreases the dose of all IV agents but also increases toxicity.

questions.question: Why is Propofol preferred as an induction agent in geriatric patients?

options.A: It has the longest duration of action
options.D: Propofol because of shorter half-life is preferred

questions.explanation: Page 243: Propofol because of shorter half-life is preferred. Decreased cardiac output may slow the onset with IV agents.

questions.question: What is the clinical significance of decreased pseudocholinesterase levels in geriatric patients?

options.A: Requires avoidance of all muscle relaxants
options.B: Prolongs the effect of suxamethonium clinically
options.C: Theoretically prolongs suxamethonium effect but clinically no prolongation is seen
options.D: Has no effect on any drug

questions.explanation: Page 243: Decreased pseudocholinesterase levels due to decreased hepatic functions may theoretically prolong the effect of suxamethonium, however clinically no prolongation has been seen.

questions.question: Why may intubation and mask ventilation become difficult in geriatric patients?

options.B: Due to edentulous jaw and decreased neck movements from RA and osteoarthritis

questions.explanation: Page 243: Intubation and mask ventilation may become difficult due to edentulous jaw and decreased neck movements (due to rheumatoid arthritis and osteoarthritis).

questions.question: What happens to stress response during surgery and anesthesia in geriatric patients?

options.B: Stress response to cope up with hemodynamic changes decreases

questions.explanation: Page 243: Stress response to cope up with hemodynamic changes during surgery and anesthesia decreases in geriatric patients.

questions.question: By how much does the MAC of inhalational agents decrease per decade in geriatric patients?
questions.explanation: Page 243: The minimum alveolar concentration (MAC) of inhalational agents decreases by 4-6% per decade in geriatric patients.

questions.question: Which is the preferred inhalational agent for geriatric patients and why?

options.B: Desflurane - because of rapid emergence and least metabolism

questions.explanation: Page 243: Desflurane because of its rapid emergence and least metabolism is the preferred inhalational agent for geriatric patients.

questions.question: By how much should opioid doses be reduced in geriatric patients?
questions.explanation: Page 243: Doses of opioids should be reduced by half in geriatric patients due to age-related decrease in drug metabolism and excretion.

questions.question: Which is the most preferred opioid for geriatric patients?
questions.explanation: Page 243: Remifentanil because of its ultra-short life is the most preferred opioid for old age patients.

questions.question: What property of Atracurium and Cis-atracurium makes them the muscle relaxants of choice for old age patients?
questions.explanation: Page 243: Hoffman degradation makes Atracurium and Cis-atracurium to be the muscle relaxants of choice for old age patients as it is independent of hepatic and renal function.

questions.question: Why can decreased cardiac output delay the onset of muscle relaxants in geriatric patients?

options.A: Due to larger volume of distribution
options.B: Decreased cardiac output delays drug delivery to the neuromuscular junction
options.D: Due to increased plasma protein binding

questions.explanation: Page 243: Decrease cardiac output can delay the onset of muscle relaxants in geriatric patients as the drug takes longer to reach the neuromuscular junction.

questions.question: What change in pain perception occurs in the postoperative period in geriatric patients?

options.A: Studies show age-related decrease in pain perception

questions.explanation: Page 243: Studies have shown that there occurs age related decrease in pain perception in geriatric patients in the postoperative period.

questions.question: What is the relationship between geriatric patients and opioid sensitivity postoperatively?

options.C: They are less sensitive to opioids
options.D: They are oversensitive to opioids and therefore opioids should be used very carefully

questions.explanation: Page 243: Geriatric patients are oversensitive to opioids, therefore opioids should be used very carefully in the postoperative period.

questions.question: Which postoperative complications are geriatric patients more prone to?

options.D: Hypoxia and urinary retention in postoperative period

questions.explanation: Page 243: Geriatric population is more prone for hypoxia and urinary retention in the postoperative period.

questions.question: What is the overall prevalence of postoperative delirium in older patients?
questions.explanation: Page 243: The overall prevalence of postoperative delirium in older patients is estimated to be 10%.

questions.question: To what percentage can postoperative delirium incidence increase in patients shifted to ICU?
questions.explanation: Page 243: The incidence of delirium can increase to more than 50% in patients shifted to ICU.

questions.question: Which age is cited as an important predisposing factor for postoperative delirium?
questions.explanation: Page 243: Important predisposing factors are age >65 years, male gender, major surgery, general anesthesia (compared to regional anesthesia), and other factors like drugs and comorbidities.

questions.question: Which type of surgery is a predisposing factor for postoperative delirium?
questions.explanation: Page 243: Important predisposing factors include major surgery (compared to minor), general anesthesia (compared to regional anesthesia), age >65 years and male gender.

questions.question: Which analgesic drug is listed as predisposing to postoperative delirium?
questions.explanation: Page 243: Drugs like meperidine (pethidine) are listed as predisposing factors for postoperative delirium in geriatric patients.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a predisposing factor for postoperative delirium?
questions.explanation: Page 243: Male gender (not female gender) is listed as a predisposing factor. Other predisposing factors include age >65 years, major surgery, GA, meperidine, cognitive impairment, dementia, comorbidities, polypharmacy, electrolyte imbalance, pain, and sleep deprivation.

questions.question: What are the short-term changes after surgery related to POCD in geriatric patients?

options.B: Short-term (up to 3 months) changes in cognitive function well-documented in 10-15% of geriatric population after surgery
options.D: Permanent cognitive decline in all patients

questions.explanation: Page 243: Short-term (up to 3 months) changes in cognitive function have been very well-documented in 10-15% of geriatric population after surgery.

questions.question: What percentage of patients can have long-term (>3 months) cognitive impairment after surgery?
questions.explanation: Page 243: Long-term (>3 months) cognitive impairment can also occur in 1% of the patients.

questions.question: What is the relationship between POCD and the type of surgery/anesthesia?

options.A: POCD is more common with general anesthesia
options.B: POCD is more common with regional anesthesia
options.C: The incidence of POCD is independent of both surgery and anesthesia
options.D: POCD is more common with major surgery

questions.explanation: Page 243: The incidence of postoperative cognitive dysfunction (POCD) is independent of both surgery and anesthesia. Studies have shown no difference in the incidence of POCD in minor versus major surgery and regional versus general anesthesia.

questions.question: What is the current treatment for POCD?

options.C: At present there is no specific treatment available for POCD

questions.explanation: Page 243: At present there is no specific treatment available for POCD.

questions.question: What change in albumin levels occurs in old age and what is its pharmacological significance?

options.B: Albumin decreases, leading to decreased unbound fraction of drug, decreasing dose requirements but increasing toxicity
options.C: Albumin increases, so more drug is protein-bound
options.D: Decreased albumin leads to decreased unbound fraction

questions.explanation: Page 243: Decreased albumin levels in old age lead to decreased unbound fraction of the drug. This along with reduced metabolism and clearance decreases the dose of all IV agents but also increases toxicity.

questions.question: What happens to the volume of distribution of drugs in geriatric patients?
questions.explanation: Page 243: Decrease volume of distribution is one of the reasons why dose of all IV agents decreases in geriatric patients, along with increased brain sensitivity, decreased albumin, and reduced metabolism.

questions.question: Why does general anesthesia have a higher incidence of postoperative delirium compared to regional anesthesia?

options.A: Due to increased stress response under GA
options.B: General anesthesia (compared to regional anesthesia) is an important predisposing factor for postoperative delirium
options.C: Due to higher opioid use under GA
options.D: Due to longer duration of surgery under GA

questions.explanation: Page 243: General anesthesia compared to regional anesthesia is listed as an important predisposing factor for postoperative delirium in geriatric patients. This is also why regional anesthesia is preferred.

questions.question: What happens to residual volume and functional residual capacity in old age due to decreased lung elasticity?

options.A: Decreased elasticity leads to alveolar distension and increase in residual volume and functional residual capacity

questions.explanation: Page 242: Decreased elasticity leads to alveolar distension and increase in residual volume and functional residual capacity in old age.

questions.question: What happens to the adrenergic response with age and what is its clinical significance?

options.C: Decreased adrenergic response, contributing to increased incidence of cardiac complications

questions.explanation: Page 242: Decrease in adrenergic response along with decreased heart rate and decreased cardiac reserve increase the incidence of cardiac complications in old age.

questions.question: Which pulmonary complication associated with general anesthesia can be avoided by using regional anesthesia in geriatric patients?

options.C: Pulmonary complications associated with general anesthesia may be avoided with regional anesthesia

questions.explanation: Page 243: Pulmonary complications associated with general anesthesia (GA) may be avoided with the use of regional anesthesia in geriatric patients. This is one of the advantages of regional anesthesia.

questions.question: What is the key statement regarding chronological age and surgery/investigations in geriatric patients?

options.A: Age > 70 requires full workup before surgery
options.B: Chronological age is neither a contraindication for any surgery nor a criterion to do battery of routine investigations
options.C: Chronological age is a contraindication for high-risk surgery

questions.explanation: Page 244 (Key Points): Chronological age is neither a contraindication for any surgery nor a criterion to do battery of routine investigations. Investigations should be guided by associated comorbidity.
